texts.full_text: though it is the second most common major surgery, thecompleted text: Even after being such widespread procedures many people do not know what exactly hysteroectomies entail and how they impact your body both physically and hormonally afterwards... What happens during this procedure remains somewhat mysterious to those who haven't experienced first-hand knowledge about its effects; however there are some things everyone should understand prior undergoing surgery: Firstly let's define precisely just where does one’s uterus reside? Your womb sits deep within pelvic cavity surrounded by ligaments connecting it firmly onto nearby structures like bladder or rectum. Secondly understanding what happens during the procedure is key, hysterectomies can be performed in several ways depending on your condition and medical history: Total abdominal (TAH), Vaginal Hysteroectomy(VH) , Laparoscopic-assisted vaginal Hysteretomy.(LAVS). TAH involves an incision being made through abdomen allowing access for removal of entire uterus along with cervix while preserving ovaries if desired by patient. VH utilizes natural birth canal instead which makes it less invasive compared to abdominal approach however might not be suitable option when large fibroids obstruct vaginal path way hence requiring Laparoscopic-assisted version where small incisions near belly button provide enough space required tools aiding success

texts.full_text: spot.[@bib0050] A bare spot has not been observed in fetal shoulders.[@bib0030] A bare spot at the center of the glenoid in a 14-year-old boy after a football injury was observed by Kim.[@bib0045] Similarly, Gagliardi and Carino[@bib0035] observed a bare spot in a 14-year-old with a traumatized shoulder; there was a 4-mm central area of hyaline cartilage loss without changes in the subchondral bone. With this in mind, the aims of this study were to evaluate the incidence of a bare spot in an adult population, to determine the length and width of the bare spot, and to assess the relationship between the bare spot and tubercle of Assaki. Material and methods {#s0010} ==================== Source of population {#s0015} -------------------- A total of 140 shoulders from 30 male and 40 female cadavers, with an average age of 81.5 years (range, 53-101 years), were dissected and examined. Dissection and measurement {#s0020} -------------------------- All muscles and blood vessels surrounding the shoulder joint as well as the fibrous capsule were inspected and then removed to expose the glenoid fossa with the glenoid labrum attached. By use of a digital caliper, the maximum length between the most superior and inferior aspects of the bare spot was taken; its maximum width was taken as the distance between the most anterior and posterior points. Statistics {#s0025} ---------- Data were double entered into the Statistical Package for Social Sciences (version 21; IBM, Armonk, NY, USA). Kruskal-Wallis and one way analysis of variance on ranks tests were used to determine statistical significance, which was set at *P* \< .05. The repeatability and reliability of the measurements taken was determined by randomly selecting shoulders from the study cohort. Three measurements were taken on 3 separate occasions by 1 individual;

texts.full_text: he research involves a combination of biochemical assays for detecting lymphocyte sensitization potency as well as histamine release capacities in cell cultures. These methodologies help pinpoint regions or entities (such as lipopolysaccharides, pili proteins) within the bacterial envelope that can activate host immune reactions leading to inflammation and other symptoms characteristic of pertussis. In parallel with morphological assessments conducted by light microscopy techniques at lower magnifications for overall cellular structures affected in infected samples versus controls over time post-infection; ultrastructural analysis using electron microscopes provides high-resolution images essential not only for observing detailed alterations but also tracking the intracellular localization and aggregation patterns associated with virulence factors during infection. This comprehensive approach seeks to provide a foundational understanding of B. pertussis biology, which may inform future therapeutic strategies targeting various stages or components involved in its disease-causing potential against immune defenses within host organisms like humans particularly vulnerable groups such as infants who lack mature adaptive immunity mechanisms effectively countered by this pathogen initially upon exposure until protective antibodies develop adequately through vaccination

texts.full_text: factual theories of knowledge and the notion of actuality Jan Heylen Abstract The central question of this article is how to combine counterfactual theories of knowledge with the notion of actuality. It is argued that the straightforward combination of thEse two elements leads to problems, viz. the problem of collapsing knowledge and the problem of missing knowledge. In other words, there is overgeneration of knowledge and there is undergeneration of knowledge. The combination of these problems cannot be solved by appealing to methods by which beliefs are formed. An alternative solution is put forward. The key is to rethink the closeness relation that is at the heart of counterfactual theories of knowledge. Keywords: Knowledge; Counterfactual belief; Safety; Sensitivity; Adherence; Belief; Actuality 1 InIntroduction Counterfactual accounts have been influential in various domains, including causation and explanation (Lewis, 1973), but their application to theories of knowledge has also gained traction. The core idea behind counterfactual theories is that knowing something involves a robustness condition: if one knows p at the actual world w0, then there must be no nearby possible worlds where not-p

texts.full_text: She exhibited her art work in several venues in Beirut and New York City and was featured in several magazines such as New York Art Magazine and Art Fuse. She believes that art comes with a responsibility andis an important tool to connect people across cultures, that there's no language barrier when we speak through our visuals. Hildos aka Hilda Abla has a rich portfolio of digital illustrations, animations, performances, and videos that she uses as platforms for her art. She is known for her work "Hildos in the City," which showcases her vibrant and imaginative depictions of urban life. Her pieces often feature whimsical creatures and fantastical elements, drawing viewers into a world where the mundane and the magical intertwine. In addition to her art, Hildos has participated in numerous events and festivals, including the International Festival of Comic Strips and the Artist Empowerment Challenge. She has also contributed to social causes, creating award-winning posters that raise awareness about important issues such as the Armenian Genocide Centennial and International Women Day. Hildos' work not only showcases her artistic talent but also her commitment to using art for positive change. She believes in the power of art to heal, inspire, and bring people together, and she continues to create works that resonate with audiences around the world. As a Lebanese-Armenian artist living in Brooklyn, Hildos draws inspiration from her diverse cultural background, blending elements from both worlds into her unique style. She is constantly exploring new ways to express herself through her art, pushing the boundaries of traditional media and digital techniques. Through her work, Hildos invites viewers to see the world through a lens of wonder and curiosity, encouraging us to find beauty

texts.full_text: a complete cDNA library. A complete cDNA library is one that contains at least one cDNA clone representing each mRNA in the cell. A method is provided in this unit for calculating the required base of a cDNA library such that the library should contain at least one cA method for calculating the required base of a cDNA library is the following: N = log2(P/F) where: * is the number of base pairs required for the library, * is the probability of not finding a specific cDNA clone in the library, * is the number of mRNAs in the cell. To ensure that the library contains at least one cDNA clone representing each mRNA in the cell, the probability of not finding a specific clone (P) is often set to 0.01 (or 1%). The number of mRNAs in the cell (F) is usually estimated to be 10,000 to 100,000. For example, if you assume that the cell contains 50,000 mRNAs and you want the probability of not finding a specific cDNA to be 0.01, you would calculate N as follows: N = log2(0.01/50,000) ≈ 15.6 This means you would need a cDNA library with a capacity to store at least 15.6 base pairs to have a 99% confidence of finding a specific cDNA clone. texts.full_text: this technique provides a flexible and efficient way to manipulate light in nanoscale structures, with potential implications for photonic integrated circuits and optical communications. The process involves focusing ultrafast laser pulses onto the surface of the silica substrate to create a localized heat source. This causes a microexplosion that etches away material and creates a waveguide structure within the glass. By precisely controlling the laser parameters (such as power, pulse duration, and scanning speed), we can manipulate the physical dimensions of the fabricated MMI waveguides. To characterize the multimode interference behavior, we use a combination of experimental measurements and simulations. The output facet of the fabricated waveguides is imaged using a microscope to observe the light distribution. By comparing this with theoretical models, we can infer the effective index profile and mode propagation properties of the MMI waveguide. Our results show that it is possible to achieve high-quality multimode interference in the synthesized silica waveguides fabricated by femtosecond laser pulses. We have successfully demonstrated splitting of single-mode light into multiple modes, with an output facet containing up to several hundred spatially distributed modes, depending on the design parameters. This technique offers significant advantages over traditional photolithographic methods for fabricating MMI waveguides. The femtosecond laser approach provides a direct and non-contact method for creating nanoscale structures without requiring additional lithography steps or post-processing. This can lead to lower costs and faster prototyping of photonic devices. Furthermore, the ability to control the mode splitting behavior allows us to design waveguides with specific applications in mind. texts.full_text: s{ t.Run(testCase.Name, func(t *testing.T){ isValid, message := ReadField(testCase.Input.FirstString, testCase.Input.SecondString, testCase.Input.SomeBool) if isValid != testCase.Expected.IsValid || message != testCase.Expected.Message { t.Errorf("Expected: %b, %b \n Got: %b, %b", testCase.Expected.IsValid, testCase.Expected.Message, isValid, message) } else { t.Logf("Expected: %b, %b \n Got: %b, %b", testCase.Expected.IsValid, testCase.Expected.Message, isValid, message) } }) } } A: As the error indicates, you need to include the type in your declaration. Since you're using anonymous types, that means you must repeat the type definition. This is, of course, super annoying: //Valid {"Test Case 1", //Missing type error struct{ FirstString string SecondString string SomeBool bool }{"FirstString", "SecondString", true}, // etc ... So what you should do is either use named types: type testInput struct{ FirstString string SecondString string SomeBool bool } type expected struct{ IsValid bool Message string } testCases := []struct { Name string Input testInput Expected expected }{ //Valid {"Test Case 1", //Missing type error testInput{"FirstString", "SecondString", true}, // etc ... Or (my preference), flatten your top-level struct, making everything far more readable: testCases := []struct { Name string InputFirstString string InputSecondString string InputSomeBool bool IsValid bool Message string }{ //Valid {"Test Case 1", "FirstString", "SecondString", true, // etc... I also strongly encourage you to use field labels in your definition, to improve readability: //

texts.full_text: exploring their knowledge on analgesics and potential fear of using opioids. The second phase was based on in‑depth interviews with 10 palliative care patients suffering from cancer pain who were reluctant to use opioids. RESULTS Of the 100 patients, 43 expressed concerns over commencing the treatment with opioids. Fear was reported more often in patients already on strong opioids, who either oWhy Patients are Afraid of Opioid Analgesics: A Study on Opioid Perception This research article investigates opiophobia and its impact among cancer patients experiencing chronic pain to identify barriers related to opioid use for effective symptom control. Researchers designed the study around a two-phase process involving 100 palliative care participants with either malignant or benign diagnoses, initially conducting surveys before interviewing ten oncology individuals hesitant about analgesics of this kind during phase II (interviews). Survey results revealed nearly half showed some apprehension towards initiating an opioid regimen; interestingly those already administered high doses also experienced fearfulness associated mostly around addiction possibilities rather than physiological adverse events.

texts.full_text: ll your soul, and with all your might." The first line of the Shema is a basic declaration of loyalty to God. Once that is accomplished, we list the things we must do for God - and the first of those to love. The remaining things, of course, are all about Torah study - keeping the Torah in mind, reciting it, teaching it, speaking of it, placing reminders of it on our body and houses. The overall structure closely echoes the blessing recited before the Shema - there, too, the introduction is about love, and the body of the paragraph is about Torah study. Of course, rather than the Shema echoing the blessing, it is the blessing which echoes the Shema, since the blessing was composed to accompany the Shema rather than vice versa. --------------- There is one difference though. The blessing talks Torah study plus about God's love for Israel. The Shema talks about Torah study plus Israel's love for God. Each contains love, but the direction of the love is reversed. texts.full_text: Bridge Meadows is that the children get to spend time with an older generation who can teach them unique things, and can offer a beneficial service. Since 50% of the elderly population in America is below the poverty line, this is a fantastic way for them to be useful once again in society and to serve a purpose. It can be advantageous to many parties involved to find more ways that the elderly can contribute to society, even after retirement because in the United States if you are not contributing to society you can easily be forgotten which is happening to much of the elderly population. You can hear more about this story here. All people in our lives — young or old — should be treated with care, and not be put somewhere if they will only be forgotten. We must begin to look past ourselves, and realize that many people are feeling neglected, and unloved. Some of the greatest stories you can be told are from your older relatives and from learning about their lives. It increases your awareness of past generations, expands your knowledge of American culture (or other cultures), and gives you another person in your life who will always be grateful for the time that you spend with them, which in turn makes you a more gracious person as well. texts.full_text: or instance: If someone plans travel north around Cairns/Port Douglas then they’ll probably experience warm temperatures year round since it’s located near equator; however, if somebody was headed south towards Melbourne/Tasmania things could get quite chilly during winter months! What Should I Pack? Depending on where you go within Australia and what time of the year (or season) will determine exactly how much clothing one should bring along for their trip. For example: If someone plans visit Sydney/Southport area then they might want pack plenty lightweight shirts/sweaters because it gets hot during summer but cool down at night; however, people traveling up north towards Cairns/Mount Isa areas may need bundle things since temperatures drop quite a bit between day & evening! Is There Anything To Do? Of course there’s always stuff going on regardless where in Australia! For instance: If someone wants go shopping then they can check out popular places like Bondi Beach or Surfers Paradise; however, people looking for adventure might consider trying their hand at surfing lessons along coastline before heading back inland towards Sydney Harbor Bridge area. texts.full_text: undertaken at two weeks and four, eight, and twelve months following the induction of diabetes. Many myelinated axons of both diabetic and control rats contained glycogen-like granules, axon-Schwann cell networks and fingerlike intrusions of myelin.In addition to these findings, diabetic rats exhibited several distinct pathological features that were more pronounced compared to the controls. These included: 1. Axonal Degeneration: Diabetic rats showed increased instances of axonal degeneration and loss, which became more prominent with longer durations of diabetes. This was evident in the form of shrunken or swollen axons, disorganized cytoskeletal elements, and accumulation of organelles like mitochondria and dense bodies. 2. Myelin Abnormalities: Diabetic nerves exhibited more frequent myelin abnormalities such as decompaction, splitting, ballooning, and infoldings. texts.full_text: of Capitalism We Don’t Talk About Author Supritha Rajan argues that self-interested competition may be the official line, but it’s far from the whole story Every society has at least one dominant story. Think of it as the official line: Here’s who we are, what we’re doing and why. But if you pay attention, you may notice other stories, too. Stories that circulate below the radar — and sometimes right out in the open — contradicting the official narrative. Most people don’t openly acknowledge them, but the signs are everywhere. Think of Catholic saints beaming their polytheistic, atavistic messages on brightly colored church windows. See the majestic Mary, whose exaltation helps to hold at bay the tensions of a monotheistic, father-centered religion officially in denial of the divine feminine. The Queen of Heaven papers over gaps in the main story, making it more appealing, more workable. The Church needs Mary. Without her intercession and mediation between humanity and God's will — which is often perceived as stern or distant due to its monotheistic structure— there would be a significant void within Catholic practice that acknowledges the complexity of human emotions towards divinity, especially those related with nurturing aspects. Similarly in our modern world economy dominated by capitalism—a system widely touted for championing self-interest and competition—the dominant narrative is one where individuals are free agents pursuing their own gain. Yet beneath this surface story lie other narratives: ones involving sacrifice that aren't often acknowledged or discussed openly but play a crucial role within the framework of capitalist societies. Capitalism's official line tells us about innovation, efficiency, personal freedom, meritocracy—where success comes to those who work hard and smart in an open market. But if we look closely at how capitalism operates on both individual lives as well as global scales, there are sacrifices that must be made for the system to function. texts.full_text: randomized trial of two treatments for mild traumatic brain injury: 1 year follow-up. The purpose of this study was to provide 1 year follow-up data to a previous paper that compared single-session (SS) treatment to treatment-as-needed (TAN) outcomes for mild traumatic brain injury (MTBI) survivors at 3 months post-injury. Participants were 105 adults with MTBI who were recruited from consecutive admissions to two hospital emergency wards, and randomly assigned to either the SS or TAN modality. No group differences in outcome were evident at 12 months post-injury. As well, improvements seen in the groups after 3 months were maintained at 12 months. As improvements betweenAs improvements between the two groups did not significantly differ at the 12-month mark, it suggests that both treatment modalities are equally effective over a longer term for MTBI survivors. The sustained benefits observed at 3 months indicate that early intervention can have lasting positive effects on recovery outcomes. This study highlights the importance of continued support and follow-up care in managing mild traumatic brain injury, regardless of whether patients receive single-session treatment or ongoing therapy as needed. Further research may be warranted to explore specific patient characteristics that could influence responsiveness to each type of intervention, thereby optimizing individualized treatment plans for MTBI survivors.
